P a g e  5 / 9  
 
2.6.2. Key Advantages of the 3-Class Model 
• Leveraging High-Density Data (The "Person" Class): Safety is paramount 
when navigating around humans. By focusing on only 3 classes, we were 
able to leverage our largest data segment—4,000+ samples of the 
"Person" class. This massive sample size ensures that the model is 
exceptionally robust at detecting humans in various poses (sitting, 
standing, walking) and lighting conditions, significantly reducing the risk of

collisions. 
• Enhanced Model Precision (Reduced Inter-Class Confusion): Deep 
learning models often struggle to distinguish between visually similar 
objects (e.g., a "Cabinet" vs. a "Wardrobe"). By merging these into a single 
"Obstacle" class, we eliminate "label noise" and inter-class confusion. This 
results in higher Mean Average Precision (mAP) for the remaining 
categories, as the model's "mental energy" is focused on only three distinct 
visual patterns.

visual patterns. 
 
• Significant Training & Inference Speedup: 
o Training: With only 3 output neurons in the classification head 
(rather than 11), the model converges significantly faster, allowing for 
more rapid iteration and hyperparameter tuning. 
o Inference: Reducing the complexity of the classification task allows 
the model to maintain higher frame rates (FPS) on edge devices, 
which is critical for providing low-latency audio feedback to a 
visually impaired user.

visually impaired user. 
 
2.6.3. Strategic Assumption for Milestone 3 
We hypothesize that for the "Action-Oriented" goals of this project, the DSAI-
Unified-Lite model will outperform the 11-class model in real-world "Path Clear" 
vs. "Path Blocked" scenarios. The simplicity of the output aligns perfectly with our 
Deterministic Navigation Logic, which primarily needs to know if a zone is 
"Empty" or "Occupied. " 
 
3. Depth Estimation Dataset Identification & Model Evaluation

3.1. Primary Dataset: NYU-Depth V2 
For the development of our depth perception module, the team has selected the 
NYU-Depth V2 dataset as our primary benchmark. This dataset consists of 1,449 
densely labeled pairs of aligned RGB and depth images across 464 distinct indoor 
scenes.
